titleStmt.title: THE ARCHIMEDEAN HEIGHT PAIRING FOR DIFFERENTIAL FORMS ON DEGENERATION OF RIEMANN SURFACES

analytic.title: THE ARCHIMEDEAN HEIGHT PAIRING FOR DIFFERENTIAL FORMS ON DEGENERATION OF RIEMANN SURFACES

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

startPage=-1, endPage=-1, consolidateCitations=0, consolidateHeader=1, consolidateFunders=0, includeRawAffiliations=false, includeRawCitations=false, includeRawCopyrights=false, generateTeiIds=false, generateTeiCoordinates=[], sentenceSegmentation=false, flavor=null

div.p: We define the Archimedean height pairing for fiberwise cohomologically trivial differential forms on a one-parameter degeneration of Riemann surfaces, and we study its asymptotic behavior. The proof relies on recent work by Dai-Yoshikawa on the asymptotics of small eigenvalues. As an application, we relate this pairing to the current-valued pairing of Filip-Tosatti, extending their construction to broader geometric settings.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Let π : X → S ≃ D be a proper surjective holomorphic map from a complex surface X to a Riemann surface isomorphic to the unit disk. We assume that π has connected fibers and X 0 := π -1 (0) is the unique singular fiber. Assume that X is equipped with a Kähler metric.

For two smooth real (1, 1)-forms α, β on X that are cohomologically trivial on smooth fibers X s := π -1 (s)(s ̸ = 0), i.e., Xs α = Xs β = 0, we define the Archimedean height pairing between them. Definition 0.1 (Archimedean height pairing, Theorem 4.1). For any s ̸ = 0, let -φ s ∈ C ∞ (X s ) be a potential for α on X s , i.e., dd c φ s + α | Xs = 0. Then the Archimedean height pairing ⟨α, β⟩ between α, β is a function on smooth locus S • := S \ {0}: ⟨α, β⟩(s) := Xs φ s β, s ̸ = 0.
Since π is a submersion on S • , ⟨α, β⟩ ∈ C ∞ (S • ).

We study its asymptotics around the singular fiber of the degeneration. Theorem 0.2 (Theorem 4.5, Corollary 4.6). There is a constant c α,β ∈ R such that ⟨α, β⟩ -c α,β log |s| 2 extends continuously to S. In particular, ⟨α, β⟩ ∈ L ∞ (S • ) if and only if ⟨α, β⟩ ∈ C 0 (S).

When X 0 is a reduced divisor and C 1 , • • • , C N are its irreducible components, the constant c α,β is determined in the following way: Let M be the intersection matrix of irreducible components of X 0 , given by (M ij ) 1≤i,j≤N = (C i • C j ) 1≤i,j≤N , and let M + be the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse of M . Then c α,β = v T α (M + ) T v β , where
div.formula: v α = C 1 α, . . . , C N α T , v β = C 1 β, . . . , C N β T .
div.p: Remark 0.3. The name Archimedean height pairing comes from Arakelov theory, e.g. in Bost's work [Bos90]. But their pairings are defined for algebraic cycles/divisors. Precisely, let D, E be two divisors on the one-parameter degeneration π : X → S of algebraic curves. Suppose D, E are of relative degree 0 and their supports do not intersect. For a divisor D, we define its Green's current g Ds on general fibers X s following [HdJ15, Equation (2.1)]. The g Ds is a distribution on X s solving the equation ∂∂g Ds + πiδ Ds = 0, where D s is the restriction of D on X s . We remark that g Ds is smooth on X s away from the support of D s . Then the Archimedean height pairing between D and E is a well-defined function on S • :
div.formula: s → ⟨D s , E s ⟩ ∞ := g Ds ([E s ]) = i a i g Ds (q i ),
div.p: where E s = i a i q i . In Holmes-de Jong's paper [HdJ15, Theorem 2.1], they derive a similar asymptotic result for the Archimedean height pairing ⟨D s , E s ⟩ ∞ on degenerations of algebraic curves.

Remark 0.4. Similar asymptotic results were obtained by Yoshikawa [Yos06] and Eriksson-Freixas-Mourougane [EFiMM18] in their study of Quillen metrics around singular fibers. Analogous behavior for the canonical heights of elliptic curves around singular fibers was established by Silverman [Sil92,Sil94a,Sil94b] and recently by DeMarco-Mavraki [DM20].

The study of the Archimedean height pairing for forms is motivated by the following dynamical scenario. Let X be a projective elliptic K3 surface with a parabolic automorphism T preserving the fibration: X X B := P 1

div.head: T π π
div.p: In Filip-Tosatti's paper [FT23, Theorem 3.2.14], they introduced the "current-valued pairing" η B (T, [α]), which is a current on B, for α a smooth closed real (1, 1)-form on X that is cohomologically trivial on smooth fibers.
The current-valued pairing can be used to characterize the limit properties of large iterations of the automorphism T . For example, they proved in [FT23,Corollary 3.2.20] that (0.1)
div.formula: lim n→∞ T n * ω n 2 = 1 2 π * η B (T, [T * ω -ω]),
div.p: where ω is a Kähler form on X.

When the singular fibers of π are reduced and irreducible, Filip-Tosatti established the continuity of potentials of η B in [FT23,Proposition 3.2.11]. In this paper, we remove their assumption on the singular fibers and prove the continuity in general.

Proposition 0.5 (Proposition 5.4). Let Aut π (X) be the group of automorphisms of X preserving the fibration. There exists a finite index subgroup G of Aut π (X) such that the current-valued pairing η B restricted on G has a continuous potential.
This proposition is proved by the following observation, which relates the Archimedean height pairing and the potential of current-valued pairing.

We use u to denote a potential of the current-valued pairing η B (T, [α]), then we have (in Equation (5.4)) u(s) = Xs f ω + ⟨α, (T * -I)ω⟩, where f ∈ C ∞ (X) and ω is a normalized Kähler form on X. The first term s → Xs f ω is well-studied by Barlet (Theorem 1.3) and is Hölder continuous. Therefore, Proposition 0.5 follows from the continuity of the Archimedean height pairing, which arises as the second term of the potential u.
Using Equation (0.1) ([FT23, Corollary 3.2.20]), we have the following result on parabolic dynamics of elliptic K3 surfaces.
Corollary 0.6 (Corollary 5.5). Let G be the finite index subgroup of Aut π (X) defined in Proposition 0.5. Then, for any Kähler form ω on X and any T ∈ G, the limit current η := lim n→∞ T n * ω

n 2 has a continuous potential. Furthermore, we prove that the convergence of currents (T n ) * ω n 2 → 1 2 π * η B (T, [T * ω -ω]) cannot be in C 0 loc (X \X 0 ). Therefore, the sequence T n * ω n 2 n≥1 provides a counterexample to Tosatti's question [Tos25, Question 1.5] when we let ω be a Ricci-flat metric.
Remark 0.7. The regularity of limit currents is also discussed in Cantat-Dujardin's recent paper [CD23b,Theorem D].

If the automorphism is hyperbolic, its associated limit current will generally have a Hölder-continuous potential. However, it is unknown whether the potential of a limit current given by a parabolic automorphism is Hölder-continuous.

Remark 0.8. Other related works on the parabolic dynamics of elliptic surfaces with general singular fibers include Duistermaat's monograph [Dui10] and Cantat-Dujardin's recent paper [CD23a,Section 3].

Here we sketch the proof of Theorem 0.2. By a standard reduction, we can assume that X 0 is reduced and α, β have a zero integral on each irreducible component of X 0 . Then it remains to show the continuity of the pairing in Theorem 4.4: We introduce the preferred potentials (Theorem 3.3) for α, β. Then by Dai-Yoshikawa's work [DY25], we are able to derive some estimates on preferred potentials (Theorem 3.4, Theorem 3.5). Together with some continuity results of fiber integral around singular fibers (Proposition 1.13, Corollary 1.14), we derive the continuity of the pairing (which is a fiber integral) in Theorem 4.4.

This article is organized as follows. In Section 1, we collect and prove some technical results. In Section 2, we study the properties of eigenfunctions with small eigenvalues on smooth fibers of a degeneration. We use Dai-Yoshikawa's work [DY25] heavily in this section. In Section 3, we introduce the notion of a preferred potential and establish its asymptotic estimates based on Section 2. In Section 4, we introduce the Archimedean height pairing and prove its asymptotics around singular fibers. In Section 5, we apply previous results to study the parabolic dynamics of K3 surfaces. In Appendix A, we prove a technical lemma on the asymptotics of a fiber integral.

div.head: Notation and conventions
div.p: On a complex manifold X, we define d c = i 4π (∂ -∂), so dd c = i 2π ∂∂. For a closed smooth differential form α on a compact differential manifold, we use [α] to denote its de Rham cohomology class.
We use D := {z ∈ C : |z| < 1} to denote the unit disk in complex plane, and

div.head: Technical Preparation
div.p: We collect and prove some technical results that will be used later.

div.head: 1.A. Laplacian on singular varieties
div.p: Let X be an algebraic variety with the induced Fubini-Study metric, and let X reg be the regular part of X.

Following Li-Tian's work [LT95, Section 4], we define the ordinary Laplacian ∆ on compactly supported smooth functions on X reg and let ∆ be its Friedrichs extension. Then we have Theorem 1.1 ([LT95, Section 4]). The Friedrichs extension ∆ defined on functions is self-adjoint on a compact variety X (without boundary) whose singular set Sing X is at least of real codimension 2.
When f ∈ W 1,2 (X reg ) is harmonic, i.e., ∆f = 0 almost everywhere, we have df = 0 and f is locally constant.
Remark 1.2. This theorem holds when X is a Kähler space, see [GPSS23, Lemma 11.2].

div.head: 1.B. Continuity of fiber integral à la Barlet
div.p: In [Bar82], Barlet derived the asymptotic expansion of the fiber integral for the degeneration of complex manifolds near singular fibers.
div.formula: Theorem 1.3 ([Bar82, Théorème 1]). Let D = {s ∈ C | |s| < 1}
div.p: , and let X be a reduced and irreducible complex analytic space of dimension n + 1. Let π : X → D be a surjective holomorphic map, and let us denote by X s the cycle π -1 (s). Let K be a compact subset of X. There exist rational numbers
div.formula: r 1 ≤ • • • ≤ r k in [0, 2) ∩ Q such that
div.p: for every C ∞ differential form φ of type (n, n) on X with support in K, the function F φ := Xs φ admits, as s → 0, an asymptotic expansion of the form:
div.formula: F φ (s) ∼ r=r 1 ,...,r k j=0,...,n (m,m ′ )∈N 2 T r,j m,m ′ (φ)s m s m ′ |s| r (log |s|) j
div.p: where T r,j m,m ′ is a (1, 1)-current on X.
Since F φ (s) does not blow up as s → 0, we have T 0,j 0,0 (φ) = 0 for j ≥ 1. Let 0 < r be the minimum of non-zero
div.formula: r 1 ≤ • • • ≤ r k in [0, 2) ∩ Q, then for 0 < a < min(r, 1), we have |F φ (s) -F φ (0)| ≤ C(φ) |s| a .
div.p: Thus F φ (s) is Hölder continuous around 0.
Remark 1.4. When the base of the fibration π is of higher dimension, Takayama extends Barlet's result in his recent paper [Tak20].

div.head: 1.C. Convergence on the singular fiber
div.p: Let π : X → D be a proper surjective holomorphic map from a complex manifold X of complex dimension p = q+1 to the unit disk. Suppose that π has connected fiber and X 0 := π -1 (0) is the unique singular fiber. We define Sing(X 0 ) := {x ∈ X 0 | dπ x = 0} and X 0,reg := X 0 \ Sing(X 0 ). Let X s := π -1 (s).
We introduce the following frequently used notion of convergence on the singular fiber.

Definition 1.5 (Convergence on the singular fiber). Under the above settings, let (s n ) n≥1 ⊂ D • be a sequence such that s n → 0 as n → ∞. Let (f sn ) n≥1 be a family of C k functions on X sn and f be a C k function on X 0,reg . Around any p ∈ X 0,reg , the map π is a locally trivial fibration by Ehresmann's theorem, so we have an open neighborhood U of p with the following diagram:
div.formula: i : U V × D ϵ D ϵ ≃ π
div.p: Then we have a family of diffeomorphisms i s : U ∩ X s → V × {s}. For s ∈ D ϵ , we define f s := (i s ) * (f s ) to be a C k function on V , and define f := (i 0 ) * (f ).

We say f sn converges to f in C k loc on X 0,reg if around any p ∈ X 0,reg , we have a coordinate chart U ≃ V × D ϵ and f sn → f in the C k loc (V ) topology. This notion of convergence does not depend on the choice of coordinate charts.

Remark 1.6. Let f : X \ X 0 → R be a continuous function. Then f can be extended to be a continuous function on X \ Sing X 0 if and only if f sn converges to a continuous function f 0 ∈ C 0 (X 0,reg ) in C 0 loc on X 0,reg for any sequence s n → 0. We shall show how the convergence on the singular fiber could imply the convergence of the fiber integral in the following.
Lemma 1.7. Suppose f sn → f in C 0 loc on X 0,reg and f is a continuous function on X 0,reg . Let ω be a Hermitian form on X. Then
div.formula: X 0,reg |f | l ω q ≤ lim sup n→∞ Xs n |f sn | l ω q for 1 ≤ l < ∞. Generally, if f sn → f in C k loc on X 0,reg , then X 0,reg |∇ r f | l ω q ≤ lim sup n→∞ Xs n |∇ r f sn | l ω q for 0 ≤ r ≤ k, 1 ≤ l < ∞.
div.p: Before the proof, we need a partition of unity tailored for our situation:
Lemma 1.8. Let X be a smooth manifold and K ⊂ X be a compact subset. Suppose
div.formula: {U i } N i=1 is a finite open covering of K.
div.p: Then there exists a collection of smooth functions
div.formula: {ρ i } N i=1 with ρ i : X → R such that: (1) supp(ρ i ) ⊂ U i for each i = 1, . . . , N . (2) 0 ≤ ρ i (x) ≤ 1 for all x ∈ X. (3) N i=1 ρ i (x) = 1 for all x ∈ K. (4) N i=1 ρ i (x) ≤ 1 for all x ∈ X. Proof. (of Lemma 1.8) Since K is compact and {U i } N i=1 is an open covering of K, the collection of open sets U = {U 1 , U 2 , • • • , U N , X \ K} is an open covering of X. Let {ρ 1 , ρ 2 , • • • , ρ N , ρ N +1 } be a partition of unity subordinate to U. Then we obtain the desired {ρ i } N i=1 . □ Proof. (of Lemma 1.7) Let U ≃ V ×D be a coordinate chart around some point in X 0,reg trivializing the fibration π : X → D. Let h be a continuous function on X compactly supported on U . Then if f sn → f in C k loc on X 0,reg , we have h sn |∇ r f sn | → h 0 |∇ r f | in the C 0 (V )-topology. In particular, we have X 0,reg h |∇ r f | l ω q = lim n→∞ Xs n h |∇ r f sn | l ω q . For any compact subset K of X 0,reg , let {U i } N
div.p: i=1 be an open covering of K by open sets in X \ Sing X 0 and we assume that each U i ≃ V i × D is a coordinate chart that trivializes the fibration π.
We then take the partition of unity {h i } N i=1 subordinate to {U i } N i=1 as in Lemma 1.8, then for each i, we have
div.formula: Xs n h i |∇ r f sn | l ω q → X 0,reg h i |∇ r f | l ω q .
div.p: Therefore, we have
div.formula: K |∇ r f | l ω q ≤ N i=1 X 0,reg h i |∇ r f | l ω q = lim n→∞ N i=1 Xs n h i |∇ r f sn | l ω q ≤ lim sup n→∞ Xs n |∇ r f sn | l ω q .
div.p: As K can be taken to be any compact subset of X 0,reg , we conclude that
div.formula: X 0,reg |∇ r f | l ω q ≤ lim sup n→∞ Xs n |∇ r f sn | l ω q .
div.p: □ If f sn in the previous lemma (Lemma 1.7) is zero in a tubular neighborhood of Sing X 0 , then we have the equality for the limit of fiber integral.

Lemma 1.9. Suppose f sn converges to f in C 0 loc on X 0,reg and there exists ϵ > 0 such that f sn , f are zero in a tubular neighborhood B(Sing X 0 , ϵ) := {p ∈ X : dist(p, Sing X 0 ) < ϵ} of Sing X 0 . Then for any smooth (q, q) form α, we have
div.formula: Xs n f sn α → X 0 f α, as n → ∞.
div.p: Proof. Let U ϵ = B(Sing X 0 , ϵ) be an open tubular neighborhood of the singular locus. Since X 0 \ U ϵ is compact and contained in the regular locus of X 0 , we can cover it by a finite number of open sets {U i } N i=1 in X \ Sing X 0 and we assume that each U i ≃ V i × D is a coordinate chart that trivializes the fibration π. Then we have an open covering U ϵ ∪ {U i } N i=1 of X 0 . Since π is proper, we can find ϵ 0 > 0 such that π -1 (D ϵ 0 ) ⊂ U ϵ ∪ ∪ N i=1 U i . We apply Lemma 1.8 to the compact set π -1 (D ϵ 0 ) and its open covering U ϵ ∪ {U i } N i=1 , then we have a collection of smooth functions {ρ i : U i → R} N i=0 (we denote U ϵ by U 0 ) such that
div.formula: • supp(ρ i ) ⊂ U i for each i = 0, 1, . . . , N , • 0 ≤ ρ i (x) ≤ 1 for all x ∈ X, • N i=0 ρ i (x) = 1 for all x ∈ π -1 (D ϵ 0 ).
div.p: Then for n ≫ 1 such that |s n | < ϵ 0 , we have
div.formula: Xs n f sn α = N i=0 Xs n ρ i f sn α = N i=0 Xs n ∩U i ρ i f sn α And X 0 f α = N i=0 X 0 ρ i f α = N i=0 X 0 ∩U i ρ i f α Since f sn , f are zero in U 0 = U ϵ , we have X 0 ∩U i ρ 0 f α = 0 = Xs n ∩U 0 ρ 0 f sn α For 1 ≤ i ≤ N , the fibration π on U i ≃ V i × D is trivial and (ρ i f s i α) | V i → (ρ i f α) | V i in C 0 loc . So Xs n ∩U i ρ i f sn α = V i (ρ i f s i α) | V i → V i (ρ i f α) | V i = X 0 ∩U i ρ i f α.
div.p: In summary, we have
div.formula: Xs n f sn α → X 0 f α, as n → ∞.

div.p: We can extend above lemma if the contribution of fiber integral around singularities is small enough, which is the following lemma.
Lemma 1.10. Suppose f sn converges to f in C 0 loc on X 0,reg and X 0 is reduced. Let ω be a Kähler metric on X.
For any ϵ > 0, we define
div.formula: I(ϵ) = lim sup n→∞ B(Sing X 0 ,ϵ)∩Xs |f sn | ω q ,
div.p: where B(Sing X 0 , ϵ) := {p ∈ X : dist(p, Sing X 0 ) < ϵ} is a tubular neighborhood of Sing X 0 . If I(ϵ) → 0 as ϵ → 0 and f is L 1 -integrable on X 0 , then for any smooth (q, q) form α, we have
div.formula: Xs n f sn α → X 0 f α, as n → ∞.
div.p: Proof. For any fixed ϵ > 0, we have a cutoff function η ϵ ∈ C ∞ (X) such that
div.formula: • 0 ≤ η ϵ ≤ 1, • η ϵ = 1 on B(Sing X 0 , ϵ/2), • η ϵ = 0 on X \ B(Sing X 0 , ϵ).
div.p: Then by Lemma 1.9, we have
div.formula: Xs n f sn (1 -η ϵ )α → X 0 f (1 -η ϵ )α, as n → ∞. Therefore, we have lim sup n→∞ Xs n f sn α - X 0 f α ≤ lim sup n→∞ Xs n f sn η ϵ α + X 0 f η ϵ α .
div.p: For the first term, we have
div.formula: Xs n f sn η ϵ α ≤ α ω q L ∞ B(Sing X 0 ,ϵ) |f sn | ω q . So lim sup n→∞ Xs n f sn η ϵ α ≤ α ω q L ∞ I(ε).
div.p: We then have
div.formula: (1.1) lim sup n→∞ Xs n f sn α - X 0 f α ≤ α ω q L ∞ I(ε) + X 0 f η ϵ α .
div.p: Note that
div.formula: X 0 f η ϵ α ≤ α ω q L ∞ B(Sing X 0 ,ϵ)∩X 0 |f | ω q ω q
div.p: and vol(B(Sing X 0 , ϵ) ∩ X 0 ) → 0 as ϵ → 0 by Lemma 1.12. So X 0 f η ϵ α → 0 as ϵ → 0 by L 1 -integrability of f α.
Letting ϵ → 0 in Equation (1.1), we obtain lim sup
div.formula: n→∞ Xs n f sn α - X 0 f α = 0.
div.p: We then conclude the result. □
We shall see that I(ϵ) → 0 is natural under certain uniform integrability conditions.

Lemma 1.11. Suppose f sn converges to f in C 0 loc on X 0,reg and X 0 is reduced. Let ω be a Kähler metric on X. If there is 1 < r ≤ ∞ and a uniform constant L < ∞ such that ∥f sn ∥ L r (Xs n ,ωs n ) < L, then for any smooth (q, q) form α, we have
div.formula: Xs n f sn α → X 0 f α, as n → ∞.
div.p: Proof. By Lemma 1.10, it suffices to check the following two conditions • The contribution of fiber integral around singularities
div.formula: I(ϵ) = lim sup n→∞ B(Sing X 0 ,ϵ)∩Xs |f sn | ω q
div.p: goes to zero as ϵ → 0.
div.formula: • The f is L 1 -integrable on X 0 .
div.p: The first condition is justified by the Hölder inequality and the volume estimate of tubular neighborhood in Lemma 1.12 lim sup n→∞ B(Sing X 0 ,ϵ)∩Xs
div.formula: |f sn | ω q ≤ lim sup n→∞ Xs n |f sn | r ω q 1/r B(Sing X 0 ,ϵ)∩Xs n ω q 1/p ≤ L lim sup n→∞ vol(B(Sing X 0 , ϵ) ∩ X sn )
div.p: ≤ LCϵ 2 (by Lemma 1.12).
The second condition follows from Lemma 1.7, we have
div.formula: ∥f ∥ L r (X 0 ) ≤ lim sup n→∞ ∥f sn ∥ L r < L for 1 < r ≤ ∞. So f ∈ L 1 (X 0 ). □
div.p: We prove the uniform bound for volume of tubular neighborhoods of singular set, which is used in previous two lemmas and is standard: Lemma 1.12 (Volume bound for singular tubular neighborhoods). Let π : X → D be a proper surjective holomorphic map from a complex manifold X of complex dimension p = q + 1 to the unit disk. Suppose that π has connected fiber and X 0 := π -1 (0) is the unique singular fiber. Suppose X 0 is reduced. Then Sing X 0 is of complex codimension ≥ 1 in X 0 .

Let ω be a Kähler metric on X, and denote by B(Sing X 0 , ϵ) the ϵ-tubular neighborhood of Sing X 0 in X with respect to the distance induced by ω. Then there exist constants C > 0 and ϵ 0 > 0 such that for all s ∈ D near 0 (including s = 0) and all 0 < ϵ < ϵ 0 , we have B(S,ϵ)∩Xs
div.formula: ω q ≤ Cϵ 2 .
div.p: Proof. Since Sing X 0 is a compact complex analytic subvariety of X 0 of complex dimension less than q -1, its 2(q -1)-dimensional Hausdorff measure is finite. Thus, there exists a constant C 1 > 0 such that for any sufficiently small ϵ > 0, the compact set Sing X 0 can be covered by a finite number of balls N (ϵ) of radius ϵ centered at points x i ∈ Sing X 0 , where N (ϵ) ≤ C 1 ϵ -2(q-1) .

The ϵ-tubular neighborhood B(Sing X 0 , ϵ) is strictly contained in the union of the concentric balls B(x i , 2ϵ). Next, we bound the volume of X s within these local balls. For any s (including s = 0), X s is a purely q-dimensional complex analytic cycle. Because ω is a Kähler metric on X, the standard Bishop-Lelong theorem (or monotonicity formulas) guarantees that the volume ratio is monotonically increasing. Thus, we take R 0 > 2ϵ 0 such that for all x ∈ S and all 2ϵ < 2ϵ 0 < R 0 , we have 1 (2ϵ) 2q B(x,2ϵ)∩Xs
div.formula: ω q ≤ 1 R 2q 0 B(x,R 0 )∩Xs ω q .
div.p: Furthermore, because dω = 0 and the fibers X s are homologous in X, the total volume of the fibers V = Xs ω q is a topological constant independent of s. We have a uniform constant C 2 and the following inequality
div.formula: B(x,2ϵ)∩Xs ω q ≤ V R 2q 0 (2ϵ) 2q := C 2 ϵ 2q .
div.p: Finally, combining the covering estimate and the local volume bound, we obtain, by the subadditivity of the volume, B(Sing X 0 ,ϵ)∩Xs
div.formula: ω q ≤ N (ϵ) i=1 B(x i ,2ϵ)∩Xs ω q ≤ N (ϵ) • C 2 ϵ 2q ≤ C 1 C 2 ϵ 2 .
div.p: Setting C = C 1 C 2 , the bound holds uniformly for all s near 0, completing the proof. □ Finally, we give a sufficient condition on the existence of limit of convergence on the singular fiber: Proposition 1.13. We work under the above settings and assume that X 0 is reduced. Let φ be a real function on X \ X 0 . We assume that φ| Xs is smooth on all regular fibers X s for s ̸ = 0, and define a family of smooth (1, 1) forms β s := dd c (φ| Xs ) on each X s for s ̸ = 0.
Let ω be a Kähler form on X. If the following conditions hold:
• Around any point of X 0,reg , we have an open neighborhood U such that the L ∞ -norms ∥tr ωs β s ∥ L ∞ (Xs∩U ) are uniformly bounded for |s| ≪ 1.
• The family of functions f s := tr ωs β s converges in C 0 loc to a continuous function f 0 on X 0,reg .
• The L 2 -norms ∥φ s ∥ L 2 (Xs,ωs) and ∥dφ s ∥ L 2 (Xs,ωs) are uniformly bounded for |s| ≪ 1.
Then for any sequence (s n ) n≥1 ⊂ D • such that s n → 0 and any 0 < a < 1, there exists a subsequence of s n (by abusing the notation, we denote the subsequence by s n itself ) such that
div.formula: • φ sn converges in C 1,a loc to a C 1,a function φ 0 on X 0,reg , • φ 0 ∈ W 2,p
div.p: loc (X 0,reg ) for any 1 < p < ∞, and φ 0 solves -∆ ω 0 φ 0 = f 0 almost everywhere on X 0,reg ,
div.formula: • φ 0 ∈ W 1,2 (X 0,reg ). • If α is a smooth (q, q)-form, then Xs n φ sn α → X 0 φ 0 α Furthermore, for two different limits φ 0 , φ ′ 0 , their difference φ 0 -φ ′ 0 is locally constant on X 0,reg .
div.p: Proof. Around any point of X 0,reg , we have an open neighborhood U such that the L ∞norms ∥tr ωs β s ∥ L ∞ (Xs∩U ) are uniformly bounded. By shrinking U , we assume U ≃ V ×D ϵ that trivializes the fibration π.

Around any point of V , we take a relatively compact coordinate ball W . Then φ s solves -∆ ωs φ s = tr ωs dd c z φ s = tr ωs β s on W. Note that ∆ ωs is a family of uniformly elliptic operators for |s| ≪ 1. By standard local L ∞ bounds for elliptic PDEs (see Gilbarg-Trudinger [GT01, Theorem 8.24]), we can shrink W to a slightly smaller domain W ⋐ W to obtain
div.formula: ∥φ s ∥ L ∞ ( W ) ≤ C ∥φ s ∥ L 2 (W ) + ∥tr ωs β s ∥ L ∞ (W ) ≤ C ∥φ s ∥ L 2 (Xs) + ∥tr ωs β s ∥ L ∞ (Xs∩U ) ,
div.p: where C is a uniform constant independent of s. By our assumptions, the right-hand side is uniformly bounded in s.
Next, by interior L p estimates for elliptic equations (see [GT01, Theorem 9.11]), for any 1 < p < ∞, we can further shrink W to W ′ to obtain
div.formula: ∥φ s ∥ W 2,p (W ′ ) ≤ C p ∥φ s ∥ L ∞ ( W ) + ∥tr ωs β s ∥ L p ( W ) ≤ C ′ p ∥φ s ∥ L ∞ ( W ) + ∥tr ωs β s ∥ L ∞ (Xs∩U ) .
div.p: Thus, ∥φ s ∥ W 2,p (W ′ ) is uniformly bounded for any p > 1.
By the Sobolev embedding theorem (Morrey's inequality), for p > 2q (where
div.formula: q = dim C X s ), W 2,p (W ′ ) embeds compactly into C 1,b (W ′ ) for 0 < b < 1 -2q/p. Since p can be chosen arbitrarily large, ∥φ s ∥ C 1,b (W ′ ) is uniformly bounded for any 0 < b < 1.
div.p: To patch these local estimates together, note that X 0,reg is second-countable, so we can choose a countable exhaustion by compact subsets
div.formula: K 1 ⋐ K 2 ⋐ • • • ⋐ X 0,reg such that ∪ m K m = X 0,reg .
div.p: For each K m , we can cover it by finitely many such charts W ′ . By the Ascoli-Arzelà theorem and diagonal argument, we can extract a single subsequence (s n ) such that φ sn converges in C 1,a loc to a limit function φ 0 ∈ C 1,a (X 0,reg ) for any given a ∈ (0, 1). Furthermore, the uniform bounds in W 2,p loc guarantee that φ 0 ∈ W 2,p loc (X 0,reg ) and that φ sn → φ 0 weakly in W 2,p loc . Since f sn := tr ωs n β sn converges in C 0 loc to the continuous limit f 0 , passing to the limit in the PDE implies that φ 0 satisfies -∆ ω 0 φ 0 = f 0 almost everywhere on X 0,reg .
By the third condition and Lemma 1.7, we have φ 0 ∈ W 1,2 (X 0,reg ). Since ∥φ s ∥ L 2 (Xs,ωs) is uniformly bounded, by Lemma 1.11 we have
div.formula: Xs n φ sn α → X 0 φ 0 α
div.p: for any smooth (q, q)-form α on X. Suppose φ 0 and φ ′ 0 are two limits arising from different subsequences of φ sn . Both solve the same equation -∆ ω 0 φ = f 0 almost everywhere on X 0,reg . So their difference
div.formula: h := φ 0 -φ ′ 0 ∈ W 2,p loc (X 0,reg
div.p: ) is a weakly harmonic function on X 0,reg with respect to ω 0 . Since h ∈ W 1,2 (X 0,reg ) globally and the singular locus of X 0 is of real codimension at least 2, by Theorem 1.1, h is locally constant. □

As a corollary, we have the following result on continuity of fiber integral Corollary 1.14. We work under the assumption and notations of Proposition 1.13. Assume that all conditions in Proposition 1.13 are satisfied.
If α is a smooth (q, q)-form such that C α = 0 for each irreducible component C of X 0 , then the fiber integral I(s) = Xs φ s α is continuous at s = 0.
Proof. We prove it by contradiction. Otherwise, we would have two sequences {s n } and {s ′ n } with s n , s ′ n → 0 such that lim n→∞
div.formula: I(s n ) ̸ = lim n→∞ I(s ′ n ).
div.p: By Proposition 1.13, we assume that
div.formula: φ sn → φ 0 , φ s ′ n → φ ′ 0 in C 1,a loc on X 0,reg , then lim n→∞ I(s n ) = X 0 φ 0 α, lim n→∞ I(s ′ n ) = X 0 φ ′ 0 α. Since φ 0 -φ ′ 0 is locally constant on X 0,reg and each irreducible component C of X 0 is connected, φ 0 -φ ′ 0 is piecewise constant on irreducible components of X 0 . Therefore, X 0 (φ 0 -φ ′ 0 )α = C constant C α = 0,
div.p: where C runs over all irreducible components of X 0 . Thus, lim n→∞ I(s n ) -lim n→∞ I(s ′ n ) = 0, which is a contradiction. □

div.head: 1.D. Smoothness of spectral projection
div.p: Let π : X → S be a proper, smooth submersion between smooth manifolds. In Demailly's expository article [Dem02], he proved the following: Lemma 1.15 ([Dem02, Lemma 10.3]). Let g be a Riemannian metric on X, and let g s be its restriction on each
div.formula: X s = π -1 (s). Then -∆ s : C ∞ (X s , R) → C ∞ (X s , R) is a smooth family of elliptic operators. Furthermore, the eigenvalues of -∆ s 0 < λ 0 (s) ≤ λ 1 (s) ≤ • • • are continuous in s.
div.p: Moreover, if λ is not in the spectrum {λ k (s 0 )} k≥0 of -∆ s 0 , the direct sum W s,λ ⊂ C ∞ (X t ) of eigenspaces of -∆ s with eigenvalues ≤ λ defines a C ∞ vector bundle (i.e., varies smoothly), in a neighborhood of s 0 .
As a direct corollary, we have Corollary 1.16. Let u be a smooth function on X and
div.formula: P s ≤λ : C ∞ (X s ) → C ∞ (X s ) be the orthogonal projection onto W s,λ .
div.p: Suppose λ is not in the spectrum {λ k (s 0 )} k≥0 of -∆ s 0 , then P s ≤λ (u) and (1-P s ≤λ )(u) =: P s >λ (u) are smooth on π -1 (U ), where U ⊂ S is an open neighborhood of s 0 .

div.head: Spectral geometry of degeneration of algebraic curves
div.p: In this section, we study the spectral geometry of the degeneration of algebraic curves, closely following the recent work of Dai-Yoshikawa [DY25]. In the first two subsections (Section 2.A, Section 2.B), we recall the tools developed in [DY25]. In the last subsection Section 2.C, we study the properties of eigenfunctions with small eigenvalues.
We introduce the setup for this section.

Setup 1. Let π : X → S ≃ D be a proper surjective holomorphic map from a complex surface X to a Riemann surface isomorphic to the unit disk. We assume that π has connected fibers and X 0 = π -1 (0) is the unique singular fiber. We define Sing(X 0 ) := {x ∈ X 0 | dπ x = 0}, X 0,reg := X 0 \ Sing(X 0 ) and
div.formula: X • := X \ X 0 . Let X s := π -1 (s).
div.p: Assume that X is equipped with a Kähler form ω, and let the corresponding metric be g X . Let g s = g X | Xs , then (X s , g s ) (s ̸ = 0) is a compact Riemann surface with a Kähler metric. We assume that vol(X s , g s ) = Xs ω s = 1.
Assume that X 0 is a reduced and reducible divisor of X. In particular, π has only isolated critical points on X 0 .

div.head: 2.A. Properties of small eigenvalues
div.p: For s ̸ = 0, let 0 < λ 1 (s) ≤ λ 2 (s) ≤ • • • be the eigenvalues of the Laplacian (with respect to g s ) □ s = ∂ * ∂ counted with multiplicities. For s = 0, we regard □ 0 as the Friedrichs extension of the Laplacian on X 0,reg = X 0 \ Sing X 0 with compact support.
Then by Yoshikawa's paper [Yos97], λ i (s) is a continuous function in s ∈ S for each i ≥ 1.
Let N = ♯{irreducible components of X 0 } = dim ker(□ 0 ), we have a precise control for small eigenvalues around 0:
div.formula: Theorem 2.1 ([DY25, Theorem 0.2]). There exist constants C 0 , C 1 > 0 such that for all s ∈ D • , C 0 log(|s| -1 ) ≤ λ 1 (s) ≤ • • • ≤ λ N -1 (s) ≤ C 1 log(|s| -1 )
div.p: In contrast to the first N -1 small eigenvalues, the remaining eigenvalues have a lower bound as s approaches 0.
Theorem 2.2 ([DY25, Theorem 3.8]). For all k ≥ 1, the function λ k (s) on S • extends to a continuous function on S. Moreover, for all k ≥ N , λ k (s) ≥ λ for a positive number λ > 0.

We call λ 1 (s), • • • , λ N -1 (s) small eigenvalues. Using Corollary 1.16 and the spectral gap of eigenvalue λ N (s) in Theorem 2.2, we define the spectral projection onto the direct sum of eigenspaces with small eigenvalues.
Lemma 2.3. We take a constant 0 < δ 0 < 1 depending only on the geometry of the degeneration and work on D
div.formula: • δ 0 . For s ∈ D • δ 0 , let W s,low ⊂ C ∞ (X s
div.p: ) be the space spanned by eigenfunctions with small eigenvalues λ 1 (s),
div.formula: • • • , λ N -1 (s). Let P s low : C ∞ (X s ) → W s,low be the orthogonal projection onto W s,low .
div.p: Then for any smooth function u on X \ X 0 , its low frequency part
div.formula: P s low (u) is smooth on π -1 (D • δ 0 ). Similarly, its high frequency part (1-P s low )(u) is also smooth on π -1 (D • δ 0 ). Proof. (of Lemma 2.
div.p: 3) Using the notation in Theorem 2.1 and Theorem 2.2, we define δ 0 by
div.formula: D • δ 0 = {s ∈ S • : C 1 log(|s| -1 ) ≤ 1 2 λ} On the punctured disk D • δ 0 , we have λ N -1 (s) ≤ C 1 log(|s| -1 ) ≤ 1 2 λ < λ N (s).
div.p: For s ∈ D • δ 0 , we have P s low = P s ≤ 3 4 λ . By Corollary 1.16, we know P s
div.formula: ≤ 3 4 λ (u) is smooth on π -1 (D • δ 0 ). □
div.p: Throughout this paper, we denote P s low (u) by u low and (1 -P s low )(u) by u high .

div.head: 2.B. Thick-thin decomposition of nearby fibers
div.p: From a geometric perspective, each nearby smooth fiber X s (s ̸ = 0) admits a thickthin decomposition. As the family degenerates, the thick part of X s converges to the regular locus of the singular fiber, X 0 \ Sing(X 0 ), while the thin part collapses to the singular points Sing(X 0 ). Since X 0 \ Sing(X 0 ) consists of N connected components, the thick part of X s correspondingly decomposes into N disjoint regions.
Following [DY25, Section 6], for each 1 ≤ i ≤ N , we construct a model function
div.formula: Υ (i)
div.p: s ∈ C ∞ (X s ) in Proposition 2.8 that serves as a smooth approximation of the characteristic function on the i-th thick component.
We construct these model functions in the following three steps:

Step 1: We construct a family of diffeomorphisms which sends the smooth part of the singular fiber to nearby smooth fibers. The idea is to pick a C ∞ complex vector field v on X\Crit(f ) satisfying f * v = ∂/∂s, and use this vector field to flow points on the singular fiber to nearby smooth fibers.

We collect useful results in [DY25, Section 6] and omit the construction. Then we have the following theorem Theorem 2.4. For s ∈ D and |s| ≪ 1. Let ν ∈ Z ≥1 be a positive integer depending on the degeneration. Let ϵ(s) = 2 |s| 1 8ν , we have a family of diffeomorphisms
div.formula: F s : X 0 \ ∪ p∈Sing X 0 B(p, ϵ(s)) → F s (X 0 \ ∪ p∈Sing X 0 B(p, ϵ(s)))(⊂ X s ) z → F s (z) ∈ X s
div.p: The family of diffeomorphisms enjoys the following properties:
(1) [DY25, Equation (6.2)] When s = 0, then F 0 : X 0 → X 0 is the identity map.
(2) [DY25, Equation (6.2)] The family F s is smooth in s.
(3) [DY25, Equation (6.3)] For z ∈ X 0 in the domain of F s , we have
div.formula: dist(F s (z), z) ≤ K |s| 3 4
div.p: (4) [DY25, Lemma 6.4] Let ω be the Kähler form in our settings, then
div.formula: ∥(F s ) * ω s -ω 0 ∥ L ∞ (X 0 \∪ p∈Sing X 0 B(p,ϵ(s))) ≤ K 2 |s| 1 2 .
div.p: (5) [DY25, Lemma 6.5] For any two functions χ, χ ′ ∈ C ∞ 0 (X 0 \ ∪ p∈Sing X 0 B(p, ϵ(s))), we have
Remark 2.5. The result in Theorem 2.4.(4) can be generalized to any differential form on X with C 1 regularity. For any smooth form α on X with C 1 -coefficients, we have
div.formula: ∥(F s ) * α s -α 0 ∥ L ∞ (X 0 \∪ p∈Sing X 0 B(p,ϵ(s))) ≤ K 2 |s| 1 2 .
div.p: The proof follows verbatim as in [DY25, Lemma 6.4].
We estimate the volume of the subset in X s that is not in the image of F s .
Corollary 2.6. Let O s be the image of
div.formula: F s inside X s . Then O s is open in X s since F s is a diffeomorphism. And for |s| ≪ 1, there is a uniform constant K 4 > 0 such that Xs\Os ω s ≤ K 4 |s| 1 4ν . Proof. Let χ s ∈ C ∞ 0 (X 0 \ ∪ p∈Sing X 0 B(p, ϵ(s))) be a test function such that • We have 0 ≤ χ s ≤ 1. • On X 0 \ ∪ p∈Sing X 0 B(p, 2ϵ(s)), χ s ≡ 1. Then we have 0 ≤ vol(X 0 ) -(χ s , χ s ) L 2 (X 0 ) ≤ p∈Sing X 0 K ′ 4 (ϵ(s)) 2 = K ′ 4 |s| 1 4ν ,
div.p: where K ′ 4 is a constant depending on the geometry of singular fiber. Since X 0 is reduced and ω is Kähler, we have vol(X 0 ) = vol(X s ). Together with Theorem 2.4.(5), we have
div.formula: 0 ≤ vol(X s ) -((F s ) * χ s , (F s ) * χ s ) L 2 (Xs) ≤ K 3 |s| 1 2 + K ′ 4 |s| 1 4ν . Note that 0 ≤ (F s ) * χ ≤ 1 and (F s ) * χ ∈ C ∞ 0 (O s ), we have Xs\Os ω s ≤ vol(X s ) -((F s ) * χ, (F s ) * χ) L 2 (Xs) ≤ K 4 |s| 1 4ν .

div.p: Step 2: We construct good test functions on the singular fiber, which will be flowed using diffeomorphisms in Theorem 2.4 to nearby fibers in Step 3 for constructing model functions.
div.formula: Let X 0 = C 1 + • • • + C N be the irreducible decomposition of the singular fiber X 0 . For each singular point p ∈ Sing X 0 ∩ C i , we fix a local coordinate system (U p , ζ), ζ = (ζ 1 , ζ 2 ) ∈ C 2 and ζ(p) = 0. We define r p (z) := ∥ζ(z)∥ = |ζ 1 | 2 + |ζ 2 | 2 .
div.p: Then we construct a family of functions on X 0 .
Lemma 2.7 ([DY25, Lemma 6.6]). For every 0 < ϵ ≪ 1, there exists a family of smooth functions χ
div.formula: (i) ϵ (1 ≤ i ≤ N ), such that (1) For 1 ≤ i ≤ N , we have χ (i) ϵ ∈ C ∞ 0 (C i \ Sing(X 0 )) (2) We have 0 ≤ χ (i) ϵ ≤ 1. On C i \ p∈Sing X 0 ∩C i U p , we have χ (i) ϵ = 1. (3) For any p ∈ Sing X 0 ∩ C i , we have χ (i) ϵ (z) = 0 if r p (z) ≤ 1 2 ϵ and χ (i) ϵ (z) = 1 if r p (z) ≥ 2 √ ϵ.
div.p: (4) We have dχ
div.formula: (i) ϵ 2 L 2 ≤ K 4 /(log ϵ -1 ) where K 4 > 0 is a constant depending only on π : X → S around 0 ∈ S.
div.p: (5) For each i, the χ (i) ϵ depends on ϵ continuously.

Step 3: We use the family of diffeomorphisms generated by flow in Step 1 and the collection of good test functions on X 0 in Step 2 to construct the collection of model functions on X s . Proposition 2.8 ([DY25, Section 6.2]). For 0 < |s| ≪ 1, there exists a family of smooth functions
div.formula: Υ (i) s ∈ C ∞ (X s ) (1 ≤ i ≤ N ), such that
div.p: (1) For any 1 ≤ i ≤ N and any s, there is a constant C > 0 depending only on the geometry of degeneration such that 0 ≤ Υ
div.formula: (i) s ≤ C. (2) For 1 ≤ i ≤ N , the function Υ (i) (s, z) := Υ (i) s (z) for 0 < |s| ≪ 1, z ∈ X s is continuous on X \ X 0 , i.e.
div.p: , the family varies continuously in s away from singular fibers.
(3) For 1 ≤ i, j ≤ N , i ̸ = j, we have Supp Υ
div.formula: (i) s ∩ Supp Υ (j) s = ∅. (4) For 1 ≤ i ≤ N , we have estimates Υ (i) s 2 L 2 (Xs) = 1 + O(|s| 1 8ν ), dΥ (i) s 2 L 2 (Xs) ≤ K log(|s| -1 )
div.p: The constant K > 0 depends only on (π : X → D, ω). (5) The function
div.formula: Υ (i) s (z) is constant for z ∈ X s in F s (X 0 \ p∈Sing X 0 B(p, 4ϵ(s) 1 2 )), where ϵ(s) = 2 |s| 1 8ν . Proof. We define (2.1) Υ (i) (s) = (F s ) * (χ (i) 4ϵ(s) )/ Area(C i ) ∈ C ∞ (X s ), where ϵ(s) = 2 |s| 1 8ν . Since 0 ≤ χ (i) ϵ(s) ≤ 1, Proposition 2.8.(1) is proved. Since χ (i)
div.p: ϵ(s) depends on s continuously by Lemma 2.7.(5) and F s is continuous in s, Υ (i) (s) varies continuously in s. Then Proposition 2.8.(2) is proved.
For the remaining parts, we refer to [DY25, Section 6.2]. □ Corollary 2.9. For any p ∈ X 0 \ Sing X 0 , there is an open neighborhood U of p such that
div.formula: Υ (i) (s, z) := Υ (i)
div.p: s (z) is constant for 1 ≤ i ≤ N . Moreover, we can extend Υ (i) (s, z) to be a continuous function on X \ Sing X 0 . And
div.formula: Υ (i) (s, z) is locally constant around X 0 \ Sing X 0 .
div.p: Proof. By the definition of F s in Theorem 2.4, for any p in X 0 \ Sing X 0 , we can take an open neighborhood
div.formula: U of p in X such that U ∩ X s is contained in F s (X 0 \ p∈Sing X 0 B(p, 4ϵ(s) 1 2 )).
div.p: From the definition of Υ (i) in Equation (2.1), the Υ (i) equals to 1/ Area(C i ) on U . So Υ (i) is locally constant around any point in X \ Sing X 0 .
Together with Proposition 2.8.(2), we know that Υ (i) is continuous on X \ Sing X 0 . Furthermore, it equals to 1/ Area(C i ) around any point in X \ Sing X 0 . □

div.head: We call Υ (i)
div.p: s model functions.

div.head: 2.C. Properties of small eigenfunctions
div.p: Eigenfunctions associated with small eigenvalues are called small eigenfunctions. We approximate these using the model functions defined in Proposition 2.8. The results are summarized below.
Theorem 2.10. Let {Φ i (s, z)} N -1 i=1 be eigenfunctions of ∆ ωs with eigenvalues
div.formula: 0 < λ 1 (s) ≤ λ 2 (s) ≤ • • • ≤ λ N -1 (s), i.e., -∆ ωs Φ i = λ i (s)Φ i , on X s . Then for each 1 ≤ j ≤ N -1, we have a decomposition for Φ j , (2.2) Φ j (s, z) = N l=1 C l(j+1) (s)Υ (l) (s, z) + R j (s, z) for 0 < |s| ≪ 1, where C ij (s) = Xs Υ (i) s Φ j-1 ω s = (Υ (i) s , Φ j-1 ) L 2 (Xs,

div.head: ωs) . And we have the following estimates
div.p: (1) There is a uniform constant C such that |C lj (s)| < C for all 1 ≤ l ≤ N and all s.
(2) There is a uniform constant C such that
div.formula: ∥dR j ∥ 2 L 2 (Xs) ≤ C 2 log(|s| -1 ) , ∥R j ∥ 2 L 2 (Xs) ≤ C 2 1 log(|s| -1 )
div.p: for all 1 ≤ j ≤ N and all s.

Remark 2.11. We mention that the choice of small eigenfunctions is not unique. Nevertheless, the non-uniqueness of choice can be controlled: Let {Φ i (s, z)} N -1 i=1 and {Φ ′ i (s, z)} N -1 i=1 be two families of normalized small eigenfunctions. They span the same vector space V and are orthonormal bases under the L 2 -inner product. Thus, they are related by an orthogonal matrix, i.e., there exists a matrix O ∈ O(N -1) such that
From the equation Equation (2.2), we have
So for another choice of eigenfunctions, the remainders are related by an orthogonal matrix and the estimates are the same for
Proof. We begin with the Fourier expansion for each model function Υ
div.formula: (i) s (1 ≤ i ≤ N ) on X s with respect to the lower frequency eigenfunctions {Φ j (s, z)} N -1 j=1 , (2.3) Υ (i) s = Xs Υ (i) s ω s + N -1 j=1 c ij (s)Φ j =:Υ (i) s,low +h (i) s ,
div.p: where

div.p: Since h

div.p: The equality in the third line holds since the high frequency term h
div.formula: (i) s in the Fourier expansion (Equation (2.3)) is L 2 (X s , ω s )-orthogonal to low frequency modes Φ j (1 ≤ j ≤ N -1).
div.p: Integrating by parts on the compact manifold X s and using the estimate for the model functions Υ (i) s (in Proposition 2.8(4)), we get
div.formula: -∆ ωs Υ (i) s , Υ (i) s L 2 (Xs) = dΥ (i) s 2 L 2 (Xs) ≤ K log(|s| -1 )
div.p: where K is a uniform constant. Since ∆ ωs preserves orthogonality in the Fourier expansion Equation (2.3), we have

div.formula: λ h (i) s 2 L 2 (Xs,ωs) ≤ K log(|s| -1 )
div.p: So we can find a uniform K 1 > 0 such that for all 1 ≤ i ≤ N , we have
div.formula: h (i) s 2 L 2 (Xs,ωs) ≤ K ′ log(|s| -1 )

div.p: We now go back to our theorem.
(1) We use the notation Φ 0 := 1 and consider the following correlation matrix

div.head: Estimates of preferred potentials
div.p: The setup for this section is the same as Section 2 (see Setup 1). Given a smooth real (1, 1)-form α on X, we introduce the following two integrability conditions:

Condition 3.1. We say α satisfies the integrability condition on general fibers, if Xs α = 0 for all s ̸ = 0. Condition 3.2. We say α satisfies the integrability condition on the singular fiber, if α satisfies the integrability condition on general fibers (Condition 3.1) and
div.formula: C i α = 0,
div.p: where C i (1 ≤ i ≤ N ) are irreducible components of the reduced singular fiber X 0 .
Our goal in this section is to study the preferred potentials of smooth (1, 1)-forms on X satisfying Condition 3.1. The notion of preferred potentials originates from [FT23, Theorem 3.2.4].

div.head: Definition 3.3 (Preferred potentials).
div.p: Let α be a smooth real (1, 1)-form on (X, ω) satisfying the integrability condition on general fibers (Condition 3.1). Then there is
div.formula: a unique φ ∈ C ∞ (X \ X 0 ) such that Xs φω s = 0, (α + dd c φ) | Xs = 0.
div.p: We say that φ is the preferred potential of the differential form α. Since (X s , ω s ) is compact and φ | Xs =: φ s is smooth on it, we can expand it using Fourier series in terms of eigenfunctions of ∆ ωs :
div.formula: φ s = N -1 i=1 c i (s)Φ i (s, z) + i≥N c i (s)Φ i (s, z).
div.p: The coefficients are determined by
div.formula: c i (s) = Xs φ s Φ i (s, z)ω s = Xs φ s -dd c z Φ i (s, z) λ i (s) = Xs dd c z (φ s ) -Φ i (s, z) λ i (s) = 1 λ i (s) Xs Φ i (s, z)α.
div.p: We use b i (s) := Xs Φ i (s, z)α to denote the integral terms, and the properties of λ i (s) are known for 1 ≤ i ≤ N -1 in Theorem 2.1.
We then divide φ into two parts, a low frequency part and a high frequency part, following the Fourier expansion:
div.formula: φ low = N -1 i=1 c i (s)Φ i (s, z), φ high = i≥N c i (s)Φ i (s, z). (3.1)
div.p: These two parts are smooth on X \ X 0 by Lemma 2.3.
Then we introduce the main theorems of this section.
Theorem 3.4. We work under Setup 1. Suppose α is a smooth real (1, 1)-form on (X, ω) with Condition 3.1. Let φ be the preferred potential of α.
(1) We have an L ∞ bound for φ high ,
div.formula: ∥φ high ∥ L ∞ (Xs) ≤ C ∥tr ωs α∥ L ∞ (Xs)
div.p: for all s ̸ = 0. The constant C only depends on the geometry of the degeneration.
(2) We have control over the growth order of ∥φ low ∥ L ∞ (Xs) ,
div.formula: ∥φ low ∥ L ∞ (Xs) ≤ C ∥tr ω 0 α∥ L 1 (X 0 ) log(|s| -1 ).
div.p: for all s ̸ = 0. The constant C only depends on the geometry of the degeneration.
When α satisfies the integrability condition on the singular fiber (Condition 3.2), the growth order of ∥φ low ∥ L ∞ (Xs) has a better control.

Theorem 3.5. We use the same assumption and notations in Theorem 3.4. We assume that the smooth real (1, 1)-form α satisfies Condition 3.2. Then the low frequency part φ low of the preferred potential satisfies
div.formula: ∥φ low ∥ L ∞ (Xs) log 1/2 (|s| -1 )

div.head: 3.A. Estimate of the high frequency part
div.p: We prove the first part of Theorem 3.4 regarding the high frequency part of the preferred potential. The proof is based on Cheng-Li's argument [CL81] (also seen in Siu's lecture note [Siu87, Appendix (A.2)]).
First, we introduce the following high frequency truncated heat kernel and Green's function on each (X s , ω s ):
div.formula: H N s (z 1 , z 2 , t) = ∞ i=N e -λ i (s)t Φ i (s, z 1 )Φ i (s, z 2 ) (truncated heat kernel) G N s (z 1 , z 2 ) = ∞ i=N 1 λ n (s) Φ i (s, z 1 )Φ i (s, z 2 ) (truncated Green's function)
div.p: Then the high frequency part φ high in Equation (3.1) can be represented by
div.formula: (3.2) φ high (z, s) = z 2 ∈Xs (tr ωs α)(z 2 )G N s (z, z 2 )ω s (z 2 )
div.p: To obtain the desired bound on φ high in Theorem 3.4, we estimate G N s (z 1 , z 2 ) using Cheng-Li's argument: Proposition 3.6. There is a uniform constant C > 0 independent of s ∈ D • such that
div.formula: G N s (z 1 , z 2 ) ≥ -C, z 1 , z 2 ∈ X s , for all s ∈ D • .
div.p: Proof. Let H s (z 1 , z 2 , t) be the heat kernel of (X s , ω s ), then we have
div.formula: (3.3) 1 V (s) + N -1 i=1 e -λ i (s)t Φ i (s, z 1 )Φ i (s, z 2 ) + H N s (z 1 , z 2 , t) = H s (z 1 , z 2 , t) ≥ 0, where V (s) = Xs ω s is the volume of X s . Since ω is closed, V (s) ≡ V is constant.
div.p: From the lower bound of higher eigenvalues in Theorem 2.2, we know that there exists λ > 0 such that λ k (s) ≥ λ for all k ≥ N . Then
div.formula: d dt H N s (z, z, t) = ∞ i=N -λ i (s)e -λ i (s)t |Φ i (s, z)| 2 ≤ -λH N s (z, z, t).
div.p: By Grönwall's inequality, we have
div.formula: H N s (z, z, t) ≤ H N s (z, z,
div.p: 1)e -λ(t-1) for t ≥ 1 By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, we get
div.formula: H N s (z 1 , z 2 , t) ≤ H N s (z 1 , z 1 , t) • H N s (z 2 , z 2 , t) ≤ H N s (z 1 , z 1 , 1) • H N s (z 2 , z 2 , 1)e -λ(t-1) for t ≥ 1. (3.4)
div.p: To estimate the truncated Green's function, we note that
div.formula: ∞ 0 H N s (z 1 , z 2 , t)dt = G N s (z 1 , z 2 ) = ∞ i=N 1 λ i (s) Φ i (s, z 1 )Φ i (s, z 2 ). Then G N s (z 1 , z 2 ) = 1 0 H N s (z 1 , z 2 , t)dt + ∞ 1 H N s (z 1 , z 2 , t)dt ≥ 1 0 H N s (z 1 , z 2 , t)dt -H N s (z 1 , z 1 , 1) • H N s (z 2 , z 2 , 1) ∞ 1 e -λ(t-1) dt = 1 0 H s (z 1 , z 2 , t) - 1 V - N -1 i=1 e -λ i (s)t Φ i (s, z 1 )Φ i (s, z 2 ) dt -I 2
div.p: (Using Equation (3.3), and
div.formula: I 2 := H N s (z 1 , z 1 , 1) • H N s (z 2 , z 2 , 1) ∞ 1 e -λ(t-1) dt) ≥ 1 0 - 1 V - N -1 i=1 e -λ i (s)t Φ i (s, z 1 )Φ i (s, z 2 ) dt -I 2
div.p: (Using the fact that the heat kernel H s (z 1 , z 2 , t) ≥ 0)
Here
It remains to control I 1 , I 2 by some uniform constants that are independent of s. We begin with basic results which relate the Sobolev constants to the heat kernel and eigenfunction bounds.
Theorem 3.7 (Theorem 2.1, [Yos97]). For a manifold (M, g) of real dimension 2, the following two inequalities are equivalent:
(1) For 0 < t ≤ 1 and (x, y) ∈ M × M ,
div.formula: H(x, y, t) ≤ C 1 t -2 (2) For every f ∈ C ∞ (M ), ∥f ∥ L 4 ≤ C 2 (∥df ∥ L 2 + ∥f ∥ L 2 )
div.p: Here C 1 and C 2 depend continuously on each other.

div.head: By classical Moser iteration, we have
div.p: Theorem 3.8 ([Pet16, Theorem 9.2.7]). Let (M, g) be a compact Riemannian manifold, with the following Sobolev inequality ∥u∥ 2ν ≤ S ∥∇u∥ 2 + ∥u∥ 2 where ν > 1. Then for ∆u = -λu, we have
Specifically, if ν = 2 in the above Sobolev inequality, and ∥u∥ 2 = 1, then for ∆u = -λu, we have
Finally, we introduce the uniform Sobolev inequality for a Kähler family.

Theorem 3.9 ([Tos10, Lemma 3.2]). Let π : (X, ω) → D be a Kähler family, i.e., π is a proper, surjective holomorphic map from a Kähler manifold (X, ω) to the unit disk D. Suppose that X 0 := π -1 (0) is the unique singular fiber. Assume dim C X s = 1. Then for 0 < |s| < 0.5, we have a uniform Sobolev constant C for (X s , ω s ), where ω s = ω| Xs :
div.formula: ∥f ∥ L 4 (Xs) ≤ C(∥df ∥ L 2 + ∥f ∥ L 2 ) ∀f ∈ C ∞ (X s )
div.p: So I 1 is bounded uniformly by Theorem 3.9 and Theorem 3.8. And I 2 is bounded uniformly by Theorem 3.9 and Theorem 3.7. This completes the proof. □
As a corollary, we can show the first part of Theorem 3.4.
Corollary 3.10. Under the settings and notation of Theorem 3.4, we have
div.formula: ∥φ high ∥ L ∞ (Xs) ≤ C ∥tr ωs α∥ L ∞ (Xs)
div.p: for all s ̸ = 0. The constant C only depends on the geometry of the degeneration.
Proof. By Equation (3.2), we have
div.formula: φ high (z, s) = z 2 ∈Xs (tr ωs α)(z 2 )G N s (z, z 2 )ω s (z 2 ) = z 2 ∈Xs (tr ωs α)(z 2 )(G N s (z, z 2 ) + C ′ )ω s (z 2 ),
div.p: where we take the constant C ′ as in Proposition 3.6 such that G N s (z, z 2 ) + C ′ ≥ 0. So
div.formula: φ high (z, s) ≤ z 2 ∈Xs |(tr ωs α)(z 2 )| (G N s (z, z 2 ) + C ′ )ω s (z 2 ) ≤ z 2 ∈Xs ∥tr ωs α∥ L ∞ (Xs) (G N s (z, z 2 ) + C ′ )ω s (z 2 ) = C ′ V ∥tr ωs α∥ L ∞ (Xs) .
div.p: Similarly, we have φ high (z, s) ≥ -C ′ V ∥tr ωs α∥ L ∞ (Xs) , where V is the volume of (X s , ω s ).
To summarize, we have ∥φ high ∥ L ∞ (Xs) ≤ C ∥tr ωs α∥ L ∞ (Xs) . □

div.head: 3.B. Estimate of the low frequency (I)
div.p: We prove the second part of Theorem 3.4 here. First, we note that
div.formula: φ low = N -1 i=1 c i (s)Φ i (s, z)
div.p: and the Φ i (s, z) are uniformly bounded by Theorem 3.9 and Theorem 3. around 0 for all 1 ≤ i ≤ N . Our tool is the decomposition of Φ i in Theorem 2.10. We recall the results here: For each 1 ≤ i ≤ N -1, we have
div.formula: Φ i (s, z) = N l=1 C li (s)Υ (l) (s, z) + R i (s, z).

div.head: And
div.p: • There is a uniform constant C such that |C li (s)| < C for all 1 ≤ l ≤ N and all s.
• The model functions Υ (l) (s, z) are discussed in Proposition 2.8. • The remainder terms R i are discussed in Theorem 2.10. Then each b i is decomposed into
div.formula: (3.5) b i (s) = N l=1 C li (s) Xs Υ (l) s α + Xs R i (s, z)α.
div.p: We have the following lemma Lemma 3.11. Under the preceding notation. For any smooth (1, 1)-form α on X,
div.formula: Xs Υ (l) s α = 1 Area(C l ) C l α + ∥α∥ ∞ O(|s| 1 4ν ) .
div.p: Proof. (of Lemma 3.11) From the construction of Υ (l) s in Proposition 2.8, it follows that
div.formula: Xs Υ (l) s α = X 0 χ (l) ϵ(s) F * s α Area(C l )
div.p: , where ϵ(s) = 2 |s| 1 8ν . Since α is a smooth form on X, by Theorem 2.4(4) and Remark 2.5, we have
div.formula: ∥F * s α -α 0 ∥ L ∞ (X 0 \∪ p∈Sing X 0 B(p,ϵ(s))) ≤ K 2 |s| 1 2 for a uniform constant K 2 > 0. So (3.6) Xs Υ (l) s α - X 0 χ (l) ϵ(s) α Area(C l ) ≤ K 2 Area(C l ) |s| 1 2 . Since χ (l) ϵ(s) is compactly supported on C l , χ(l)
div.p: ϵ(s) -1 is bounded by 2 and it vanishes on C l except on a set of measure O(|s| 1 4ν ) (by Lemma 2.7) , we have (3.7)
div.formula: X 0 χ (l) ϵ(s) α Area(C l ) - C l α Area(C l ) = ∥α∥ ∞ O(|s| 1 4ν ).
div.p: Combining Equation (3.6) and Equation (3.7), we obtain
div.formula: Xs Υ (l) s α - 1 Area(C l ) C l α = ∥α∥ ∞ O(|s| 1 4ν

div.p: On the other hand, from Theorem 2.10(2), we have
div.formula: Xs R i (s, z)α = ∥tr ω α∥ L 2 (Xs) O(log -1 2 (|s| -1 )).
div.p: Putting all these asymptotic results into Equation (3.5), we have
div.formula: b i (s) = N l=1 C li (s) Area(C l ) C l α + N l=1 C li (s) Area(C l ) O(|s| 1 4ν ) + ∥tr ω α∥ L 2 (Xs) O(log -1 2 (|s| -1 )) = N l=1 C li (s) Area(C l ) C l α + O(log -1 2 (|s| -1 )) = O(1) ∥tr ω 0 α∥ L 1 (X 0 ) + O(log -1 2 (|s| -1 )) ∥tr ω α∥ L 2 (Xs) .
div.p: We then prove the second part of Theorem 3.4.
Proof. (of Theorem 3.4.( 2)) For α whose integral is zero on general fibers, following Equation (3.8), we have b
div.formula: i (s) = O(log -1 2 (|s| -1 )) ∥tr ω α∥ L 2 (Xs) . Since φ low = N -1 i=1 c i (s)Φ i (s, z) and c i = b i /λ i , we have c i (s) = O(log(|s| -1 )) ∥tr ω 0 α∥ L 1 (X 0 ) + O(log 1 2 (|s| -1 )) ∥tr ω α∥ L 2 (Xs) .
div.p: Since Φ i (s, z) is uniformly bounded for 1 ≤ i ≤ N -1, we have
div.formula: ∥φ low ∥ L ∞ (Xs) ≤ C ∥tr ω 0 α∥ L 1 (X 0 ) log(|s| -1 ). □

div.head: 3.C. Estimate of the low frequency (II)
div.p: If α additionally satisfies Condition 3.2 on the singular fiber (i.e., α has zero integral on each irreducible component of the singular fiber), we obtain a better estimate on its preferred potential (Theorem 3.5). We prove the theorem here.
Following the same strategy as in Section 3.B, it suffices to estimate each term in the expansion Equation (3.5) of b i (s):
div.formula: b i (s) = N l=1 C li (s) Xs Υ (l) s α + Xs R i (s, z)α.
div.p: The first part is good enough when α has zero integral on each irreducible component of the singular fiber as proved in Lemma 3.11, and we have
div.formula: N l=1 C li (s) Xs Υ (l) s α = O(|s| 1 4ν ).
div.p: It remains to estimate Xs R i (s, z)α under conditions Condition 3.2, which is the following proposition. Proposition 3.12. For s ̸ = 0, we define R i := log 1 2 (|s| -1 )R i . If α is a smooth (1, 1)form on X with Condition 3.2, we then have
div.formula: log 1 2 (|s| -1 ) Xs R i (s, z)α = Xs R i (s, z)α → 0 as s → 0.
div.p: Proof. We prove it by contradiction. Suppose we have a sequence s n → 0 such that
div.formula: Xs n R i (s n , z)α ≥ ϵ 0 for some ϵ 0 > 0.
div.p: First, we check that the family of functions R i,s (z) = R i (s, z) ∈ C ∞ (X s ) satisfies the conditions in Proposition 1.13. We denote dd c z R i,s (z) by β s . • For the first condition in Proposition 1.13, we use the decomposition formula for eigenfunctions Φ i in Equation (2.2):
div.formula: Φ i (s, z) = N l=1 C l(i+1) (s)Υ (l) (s, z) + R i (s, z).
div.p: Around any x ∈ X 0,reg , we can take an open neighborhood U of x such that Υ (l) (s, z) is constant on U by Corollary 2.9. Then on U ∩ X s , we have
div.formula: dd c z R i,s (z) = β s = dd c z Φ i (s, z) = -λ i (s)Φ i (s, z)ω s . So ∥tr ωs β s ∥ L ∞ (U ∩Xs) = ∥λ i (s)Φ i (s, z)∥ L ∞ (U ∩Xs) is uniformly bounded in s.
div.p: • For the second condition in Proposition 1.13, we note that
div.formula: ∥tr ωs β s ∥ C 0 (U ∩Xs) = λ i (s) ∥Φ i ∥ C 0 (U ∩Xs) → 0.
div.p: So tr ωs β s → 0 = f 0 in C 0 loc on X 0,reg . • For the third condition in Proposition 1.13, it is proved in Theorem 2.10(3) that there is a uniform constant C > 0 such that
div.formula: ∥dR j ∥ 2 L 2 (Xs) ≤ C 2 log(|s| -1 ) , ∥R j ∥ 2 L 2 (Xs) ≤ C 2 1 log(|s| -1 )
div.p: for all 1 ≤ j ≤ N and all s. So
div.formula: R i,s(z)
div.p: L 2 (Xs,ωs)
div.formula: , d R i,s(z)
div.p: L 2 (Xs,ωs)
We then apply Proposition 1.13 to the family of functions R i,s (z) and sequence s n → 0, we then have a subsequence s kn such that
div.formula: • R i,s kn (z) converges to R 0 (z) in C 1,a loc on X 0,reg for any 0 < a < 1, • R 0 (z) ∈ W 2,p loc for all 1 < p < ∞ and it solves -∆ ω 0 R 0 (z) = f 0 = 0 a.e. on X 0,reg , • R 0 (z) ∈ W 1,2 (X 0,reg ), • Xs kn R i,s kn (z)α → X 0 R 0 (z)α.
div.p: Since R 0 (z) ∈ W 1,2 (X 0 ) and it is harmonic on X 0,reg , it is locally constant and is constant on each irreducible component of X 0 . So we have X 0 R 0 (z)α = 0. Thus the initial assumption Xs n R i (s n , z)α ≥ ϵ 0 for all n and an ϵ 0 > 0 contradicts that
div.formula: Xs kn R i,s kn (z)α → X 0 R 0 (z)α = 0.
div.p: This completes the proof. □
As a direct consequence, we can prove Theorem 3.5
Proof. (of Theorem 3.5) Following previous discussion, we have
div.formula: b i (s) = O(|s| 1 4ν ) + o(log -1 2 (|s| -1 )). So c i (s) = b i (s) λ i (s) = o(log 1 2 (|s| -1 )) for 1 ≤ i ≤ N -1. It implies that ∥φ low ∥ L ∞ (Xs)

div.head: Asymptotics of Archimedean height pairing
div.p: As an application of previous estimates, we introduce the Archimedean height pairing for smooth real (1, 1)-forms on X. We continue to use the setup from Setup 1, except in Corollary 4.6, where we allow for general singular fibers of the degeneration. Definition 4.1 (Archimedean height pairing). Let α, β be two smooth real (1, 1)-forms on X with Xs α = Xs β = 0 for s ̸ = 0 (Condition 3.1). Let φ be the preferred potential of α. We define the Archimedean height pairing ⟨α, β⟩ to be a function on
div.formula: D • ⟨α, β⟩(s) := Xs φβ, s ∈ D • . It is smooth on D • since π : X • (:= X \ X 0 ) → D • is a submersion and φ is smooth.
div.p: Remark 4.2. We can use any potential for α on X s to define the Archimedean height pairing. Actually, for any potential -φ ′ of α on X s (i.e., dd c φ ′ + α = 0 on X s ), the difference φ ′ -φ is constant on X s , where φ is the preferred potential of α. So
div.formula: Xs (φ ′ -φ)β = constant • Xs β = 0 and Xs φ ′ β = Xs φβ = ⟨α, β⟩(s).
div.p: We now state some basic properties of the Archimedean height pairing defined above. Proposition 4.3. Let α, β be two forms as in Theorem 4.1. We have (1) The Archimedean height pairing is symmetric and bilinear.
(2) The Archimedean height pairing is positive definite, i.e., ⟨α, α⟩ is a non-negative function on D • , and ⟨α, α⟩ = 0 if and only if α | Xs = 0 for s ̸ = 0.
Proof.
(1) Since the preferred potentials depend linearly on the differential forms, we have that the Archimedean height pairing is bilinear. It is symmetric because ⟨α, β⟩(s) =
Xs φ(-dd c φ ′ ) = Xs φ ′ (-dd c φ) = ⟨β, α⟩(s), where φ, φ ′ are preferred potentials for α, β respectively.

(2) We have ⟨α, α⟩(s) = Xs φ(-dd c φ) = ∥dφ∥ 2 L 2 (Xs) ≥ 0. If ⟨α, α⟩ = 0, then the preferred potential φ of α is fiberwise constant, so α| Xs = 0 for s ̸ = 0. □ Theorem 4.4 (Continuity of pairing). Let α, β be two smooth real (1, 1)-forms on X with Condition 3.1 satisfying
div.formula: C i α = C i β = 0,
div.p: where the C i are the irreducible components of the reduced singular fiber X 0 (Condition 3.2).
Then the pairing function ⟨α, β⟩ ∈ C 0 (D • ) (in Theorem 4.1) can be continuously extended to D.
Proof. (of Theorem 4.4) Let φ, ρ ∈ C ∞ (X \ X 0 ) be preferred potentials of α and β respectively.
By Lemma 2.3 and shrinking the base, we decompose φ into its low frequency part
div.formula: φ low ∈ C ∞ (X \ X 0 ) and high frequency part φ high ∈ C ∞ (X \ X 0 ). Then ⟨α, β⟩(s) = - Xs (φ low + φ high )dd c ρ = - Xs ρdd c φ low - Xs φ high β
div.p: Using the asymptotic results from Theorem 3.5, we have ρ = o(log 1 2 (|s| -1 )) and φ low = o(log 1 2 (|s| -1 )). Since φ low is in the space spanned by eigenfunctions with low frequency, we have
div.formula: ∥tr ωs dd c φ low ∥ L ∞ (Xs) = ∥∆ ωs φ low ∥ L ∞ (Xs) = o(log -1 2 (|s| -1 )).
div.p: So Xs ρdd c φ low = o(1) and it is continuous at 0. To show that Xs φ high β can be continuously extended to D, we use Corollary 1.14. It suffices to check that φ high,s ∈ C ∞ (X s ) satisfies the conditions in Proposition 1.13. Note that dd c z φ high,s (z) = -α -dd c z φ low,s (z) • For the first condition, we have
div.formula: ∥tr ωs dd c z φ high,s (z)∥ L ∞ (Xs) ≤ ∥tr ωs α∥ L ∞ (Xs) + ∥tr ωs dd c z φ low,s ∥ L ∞ (Xs) ≤ ∥tr ωs α∥ L ∞ (Xs) + C log -1 (|s| -1 ) ∥φ low,s ∥ L ∞ (Xs) ≤ ∥tr ωs α∥ L ∞ (Xs) + o(log -1 2 (|s| -1 )) So ∥tr ωs dd c z φ high,s (z)∥ L ∞ (Xs) is uniformly bounded in s. • Since tr ωs α is continuous on X \ Sing X 0 and ∥tr ωs dd c z φ high,s (z) -tr ωs α∥ L ∞ (Xs) = ∥∆ ωs φ low,s ∥ L ∞ (Xs) = o(log -1 2 (|s| -1 )).
div.p: We have tr ωs dd c z φ high,s → tr ω 0 α in C 0 loc on X 0,reg , which is the second condition. • Since ∥φ high ∥ L ∞ (Xs) (by Theorem 3.4) and ∥tr ωs dd c z φ high,s (z)∥ L ∞ (Xs) are uniformly bounded, ∥φ high ∥ L 2 (Xs) and ∥dφ high ∥ L 2 (Xs) are also uniformly bounded. So the third condition is verified.
So we can apply Corollary 1.14 to φ high,s and Xs φ high β is then continuous at 0. In summary, ⟨α, β⟩(s) can be continuously extended to D. □
For general smooth classes α and β satisfying Condition 3.1, we can extend Theorem 4.4.

Theorem 4.5 (Asymptotics of pairing). Let α and β be two smooth real (1, 1)-forms whose integrals on general fibers are 0. Then there is a constant c α,β such that ⟨α, β⟩ -c α,β log |s| 2 extends continuously to D.
The constant c α,β is determined in the following way: Let M be the intersection matrix of irreducible components of the singular fiber X 0 , given by
div.formula: M ij = C i • C j . Let M + be the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse of M . Then c α,β = v T α (M + ) T v β ,
div.p: where
div.formula: v α = C 1 α, . . . , C N α T , v β = C 1 β, . . . , C N β T .
div.p: In particular, if either α or β satisfies the integrability condition on the singular fiber (Condition 3.2), then c α,β = 0 because either v α or v β is zero.
Proof. Since the singular fiber is reduced, we have X 0 = N i=1 C i , where C i are the irreducible components.
We consider the N ×N intersection matrix M with entries
div.formula: M ij = C i •C j . By Zariski's Lemma (see [BHPVdV04, III.8.2, p.111]), we have • The matrix M is negative semi-definite. • The kernel of M is exactly one-dimensional and is spanned by 1 = (1, 1, • • • , 1). Let V 0 = x ∈ R N : N i=1
div.p: x i = 0 be the degree-zero subspace. Then M | V 0 is strictly negative definite and is invertible. To compute the inverse of M | V 0 , we introduce M + the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse of M . Then M + acts as the inverse of M on V 0 and annihilates span(1).
For each C i , the irreducible component of X 0 , we consider the line bundle L i = O(C i ) equipped with a Hermitian metric h i . Then we have a closed smooth (1, 1)-form
div.formula: γ i = -dd c log ∥e∥ 2 h i representing the class [C i ]
div.p: , where e is a local frame of the holomorphic line bundle L i . Let σ i be the canonical section of L i which vanishes exactly on C i . Then the preferred potential for γ i on each smooth fiber X s is log ∥σ i ∥ 2 h i up to a constant. We define the following map, which maps onto differential forms representing cohomology classes spanned by {[C i ]}: Form : R N → {smooth closed (1, 1)-forms with zero integral on the singular fiber}
div.formula: x = (x 1 , • • • , x N ) → N i=1 x i γ i =: Form(x)
div.p: Furthermore, the preferred potential of Form(x) on X s will be N i=1 x i log ∥σ i ∥ 2 h i up to a constant depending on s ̸ = 0.
For smooth (1, 1)-forms α, β on X such that Xs α = Xs β = 0 for s ̸ = 0, the vectors of component integrals
div.formula: v α = C 1 α, . . . , C N α T , v β = C 1 β, . . . , C N β T .
div.p: are in V 0 . We can then decompose α, β into
div.formula: α = Form(M + v α ) + (α -Form(M + v α )) =:α 0 β = Form(M + v β ) + (β -Form(M + v β )) =:β 0 Note that for each C i , C i α 0 = i-th coordinate of (v α -M M + v α ) = 0 (v α ∈ V 0 , so M M + v α = v α ).
div.p: Similarly, we have C i β 0 = 0 for each C i .
Since the pairing ⟨α, β⟩ is bilinear and symmetric, we have
div.formula: ⟨α, β⟩ = ⟨α 0 , β 0 ⟩ + ⟨α 0 , Form(M + v β )⟩ + ⟨Form(M + v α ), β 0 ⟩ + ⟨Form(M + v α ), Form(M + v β )⟩
div.p: By the previous theorem (Theorem 4.4), ⟨α 0 , β 0 ⟩ can be extended continuously to S.
Since the preferred potential for Form(M
div.formula: + v α ) is N i=1 (M + v α ) i log ∥σ i ∥ 2 h i up to a constant on X s , we have ⟨Form(M + v α ), β 0 ⟩ = N i=1 Xs (M + v α ) i log ∥σ i ∥ 2 h i β 0
div.p: Using the asymptotics of log-norm integral (Lemma A.1), each Xs log ∥σ i ∥ 2 h i β 0 extends continuously on S. Thus, ⟨Form(M + v α ), β 0 ⟩ extends Hölder continuously to S.
Similarly, ⟨α 0 , Form(M + v β )⟩ extends Hölder continuously to S.
For the last term, we have
div.formula: ⟨Form(M + v α ), Form(M + v β )⟩ = N i=1 N j=1 (M + v α ) i (M + v β ) j Xs log ∥σ i ∥ 2 h i γ j .
div.p: By Lemma A.1, Xs log ∥σ i ∥ 2 h i γ j -C i γ j log |s| 2 can be extended Hölder continuously through the origin, so
div.formula: ⟨Form(M + v α ), Form(M + v β )⟩ - 1≤i,j≤N (M + v α ) i (M + v β ) j C i γ j =:c α,β log |s| 2
div.p: extends Hölder continuously on S. Here c α,β can be simplified as
div.formula: c α,β = 1≤i,j≤N (M + v α ) i (M + v β ) j M ij = (M + v α ) T M M + v β = (M + v α ) T v β
div.p: In summary, ⟨α, β⟩ -c α,β log |s| 2 extends continuously to D. □

We note that the height pairing ⟨α, β⟩ is well-behaved under semi-stable reduction. So we can extend the asymptotic result in Theorem 4.5 to the degeneration of algebraic curves with non-reduced singular fibers.

Corollary 4.6. Let π : X → D be a degeneration of algebraic curves. Assume X is smooth, and allow the singular fiber X 0 to be non-reduced and non-irreducible. Let p : Y → D be a semi-stable reduction of (X, X 0 ) with the following commutative diagram For 0 ̸ = t 0 ∈ D t , we have an isomorphism µ t 0 : Y t 0 → X f d (t 0 ) . Let -φ be a potential for α on X f d (t 0 ) , i.e., dd c φ + α = 0 on X f d (t 0 ) . Then -µ * φ is a potential for µ * α on Y t 0 . So ⟨µ * α, µ * β⟩(t 0 ) = Question 4.7. We consider a degeneration of algebraic curves with reduced singular fibers as in Setup 1. Let α, β be two smooth real (1, 1)-forms on X satisfying C i α = C i β = 0, where C i are irreducible components of the reduced singular fiber X 0 . Is the pairing ⟨α, β⟩ a Hölder continuous function?

If this question is answered positively, we can strengthen the results in Theorem 4.5, Corollary 4.6, Proposition 5.4 and Corollary 5.5 by replacing the word " continuously" with " Hölder continuously".

div.head: Application to K3 surfaces with parabolic automorphisms
div.p: As an application of our previous results, we study the parabolic dynamics of elliptic K3 surfaces, following [FT23, Section 3]. Let π : X → P 1 =: B be a projective elliptic K3 surface. A parabolic automorphism T of X is an automorphism of X which preserves the fibration π and is of infinite order. This yields the following commutative diagram:
div.formula: X X B := P 1 T π π
div.p: In some literature (e.g. [Fil22, 6.2.2]), a parabolic automorphism is also called a twist automorphism.
Compared to [FT23], we allow possibly non-irreducible and non-reduced singular fibers of π : X → B in the setup of this section.
We use B • to denote the smooth locus of the fibration π, and we use s to denote the local coordinate on B. We use X s := π -1 (s).

div.head: 5.A. Preliminaries
div.p: Before delving deeper, we introduce basic notions and lemmas in this part. Let [E] ∈ NS(X) be the class of a general fiber of π in the Néron-Severi group of X. We consider the group Aut π (X) consisting of automorphisms of X preserving the elliptic fibration. The following filtration of NS(X) is preserved by Aut π (X):

where [E] ⊥ is the orthogonal complement of [E] with respect to the intersection form in NS(X). By the Hodge index theorem, the intersection form on the quotient lattice L := [E] ⊥ /[E] is strictly negative definite.

Let Isom(L) be the group of automorphisms of the lattice L that preserve the induced intersection form. Since the induced intersection form is strictly negative-definite, Isom(L) is a discrete subgroup of the compact orthogonal group O(rank (L)). Thus, Isom(L) is finite.
Since Aut π (X) preserves the lattice L equipped with the intersection form, we have the induced group homomorphism:
div.formula: ρ : Aut π (X) → Isom(L).
div.p: We define ker(ρ) =: Aut • π (X), which is the subgroup of Aut π (X) whose induced action on
div.formula: [E] ⊥ /[E] is trivial. Because Isom(L) is finite, Aut • π (X) is a finite index subgroup of Aut π (X).
div.p: If we work inside H 1,1 (X, R), we have a similar construction of Aut • π (X).
Lemma 5.1.
div.formula: Let [E] ⊥ H 1,1 denote the orthogonal complement of [E] in H 1,1 (X, R).
div.p: Then the following filtration is preserved by Aut π (X): R). We define Aut •,(1,1) π (X) as the subgroup of Aut π (X) that acts trivially on the quotient space
div.formula: [E] ⊥ H 1,1 /[E] R . Then Aut •,(1,1) π (X) is a finite index subgroup of Aut π (X).
div.p: Proof. Since the intersection form is non-degenerate, we have the following orthogonal decomposition H
where T 1,1 (X) R is the degree (1, 1)-part of the real transcendental classes T (X) R , and T (X) is the transcendental lattice of X (see [Huy16, Chapter 3, Lemma 3.1]).
Note that [E] ∈ NS(X), so it is perpendicular to transcendental classes T
div.formula: 1,1 (X) R . Thus, [E] ⊥ H 1,1 has a similar decomposition [E] ⊥ H 1,1 = [E] ⊥ R ⊕ T 1,1(
div.p: X) R , which descends to the quotient space as:
Since Aut π (X) preserves the transcendental lattice T (X) with its Hodge structure, the image of the induced group homomorphism:
div.formula: ρ T : Aut π (X) → Isom(T (X) R ) is a finite group by [Huy16, Chapter 3, Corollary 3.4]. Thus ker(ρ T ) is a finite index subgroup of Aut π (X). Since T 1,1 (X) R ⊂ T (X) R , we have ker(ρ T ) ∩ Aut • π (X) ⊂ Aut •,(1,1) π (X).
div.p: Since ker(ρ T ) and Aut • π (X) are finite index subgroups, Aut •,(1,1) π (X) is also a finite index subgroup of Aut π (X). □ Remark 5.2. Lemma 5.1 also holds for general projective elliptic surfaces. To see this, it suffices to show that the image of the induced homomorphism ρ T : Aut π (X) → Isom(T (X) R ) is a finite group. This follows from the fact that Aut π (X) preserves the integral lattice T (X) along with its Hodge structure, and the intersection form on the (1, 1)-part T 1,1 (X) R is strictly negative definite. (See also the proof of [Huy16, Chapter 3, Corollary 3.4].)

Let r be the index of Aut •,(1,1) π (X) inside Aut π (X). By elementary group theory, we have T r! ∈ Aut •,(1,1) π (X) for any T ∈ Aut π (X). Lastly, we introduce a useful formula on cohomology classes, which follows directly from the definition of Aut •,(1,1) π (X).
Lemma 5.3. Let v be a real (1, 1)-class in the cohomology group H 1,1 (X). Suppose v • [E] = 0. Then for any T ∈ Aut •,(1,1) π (X), we have
div.formula: T * v -v = c(v, T )[E],
div.p: where c(v, T ) ∈ R depends linearly on v.

div.head: 5.B. Current-valued pairing
div.p: We recall the construction of current-valued pairing from [FT23, Theorem 3.2.14]. We relate it to the Archimedean height pairing of smooth forms in Theorem 4.1.
Let α be a closed smooth real (1, 1)-form on X such that Xs α = 0 for s ̸ = 0. Let φ ∈ C ∞ (X • ) be the preferred potential of α.

div.head: Cohomologically, we have [α]
div.p: • [E] = 0. Using Lemma 5.3, we obtain
for T ∈ Aut •,(1,1) π (X). Note that [E] can be represented by the smooth form π * β, where β is a suitable smooth form on the base B = P 1 . We can take an f ∈ C ∞ (X) such that (5.1)
div.formula: T * α -α = π * β + dd c f. Then f + T * φ -φ is constant on each X s for s ∈ B • . Therefore, there is a function u on B • such that (5.2) π * u = f + T * φ -φ.
div.p: Let ω be a Kähler metric on X normalized such that [ω] • [E] = 1. Then u(s) can be determined by
div.formula: (5.3) Xs (π * u)ω s = u(s) = Xs (f + T * φ -φ)ω s
div.p: We call u the limit potential. We rewrite Equation (5.3) using the Archimedean height pairing in Theorem 4.1:
div.formula: (5.4) u(s) = Xs (f + T * φ -φ)ω s = Xs f ω s + ⟨α, (T * -I)ω⟩
div.p: The current-valued pairing is defined as η
div.formula: B (T, [α]) := β + dd c u, which is a current on B. It is smooth on B • since u ∈ C ∞ (B • ).
div.p: We now generalize Filip-Tosatti's current-valued pairing by allowing arbitrary singular fibers of the elliptic fibration π : X → B.

Proposition 5.4. Let T ∈ Aut •,(1,1) π (X), where Aut •,(1,1) π (X) is a finite index subgroup of Aut π (X) defined in Lemma 5.1. Then the current-valued pairing η B (T, [α]) has a continuous potential for any closed smooth real (1, 1)-form α on X such that Xs α = 0 for s ̸ = 0.
Proof. We first show that u ∈ L ∞ (B • ) following Tate's argument in [Tat83] (see also [FT23, Remark 3.2.14]).
From Equation (5.2), for any integer i ≥ 0, we have
div.formula: T i * π * u = π * u = T i * (f + T * φ -φ) = T i * f + (T i+1 * -T * )φ. Then for k ≥ 1 the Birkhoff sum S k (T, f ) = k-1 i=0 T i * f satisfies S k (T, f ) = kπ * u + φ -T k * φ. Thus, for s ∈ B • and every k ≥ 1, we have |u(s)| ≤ 1 k sup Xs |S k (T, f )| + 1 k sup Xs φ -T k * φ ≤ ∥f ∥ L ∞ (X) + 2 k ∥φ∥ L ∞ (Xs) . Fixing s ∈ B • and letting k → ∞, we have |u(s)| ≤ ∥f ∥ L ∞ (X) . Therefore, u ∈ L ∞ (B • ).
div.p: On the other hand, from the relation between the potential u and the Archimedean height pairing in Equation (5.4), the Archimedean height pairing ⟨α, (T * -I)ω⟩(s) = u(s) -
div.formula: Xs f ω s ∈ L ∞ (B • ) is bounded.
div.p: By Corollary 4.6, ⟨α, (T * -I)ω⟩ can be extended continuously on B because it is bounded and has no singularity. Using Barlet's result in Theorem 1.3, the function s → Xs f ω s is Hölder continuous on B.
In summary, u(s) = ⟨α, (T * -I)ω⟩(s) + Xs f ω s is continuous on B. So η B (T, [α]) has a continuous potential. □
As a corollary, we have Corollary 5.5. Let ω be a Kähler metric on X. For any T ∈ Aut •,(1,1) π (X), the limit current
div.formula: lim n→∞ (T n ) * ω n 2 = 1 2 π * η B (T, [T * ω -ω]).
div.p: has a continuous potential. We note that the convergence of currents
div.formula: (T n ) * ω n 2 → 1 2 π * η B (T, [T * ω -ω]) cannot be in C 0 loc (X \ X 0 ).
div.p: In particular, let ω be a Ricci flat Kähler metric on the K3 surface X, i.e.,
div.formula: ω 2 = vol(ω)Ω ∧ Ω,
div.p: where Ω is the normalized holomorphic (2, 0)-form on X. Then
div.formula: (T n ) * ω n 2 = 1 2 π * η B (T, [T * ω -ω]). So lim n→∞ (T n ) * ω n 2
div.p: has continuous potential by Proposition 5.4. We now prove that the convergence of currents
div.formula: (T n ) * ω n 2 → 1 2 π * η B (T, [T * ω -ω]) is not a C 0 loc (X \ X 0 ) convergence. We proceed by contradiction, assuming that (T n ) * ω n 2 → 1 2 π * η B (T, [T * ω -ω]) in C 0 loc (X \ X 0 ). We let ξ = T * ω -ω.
div.p: Then by elementary computation (see also [FT23, Proposition 3.2.15]), we have
div.formula: T n * ω = ω + nξ + dd c (nφ -S n (T, φ)) + n(n -1) 2 π * η B (T, [ξ]),
div.p: where φ is the preferred potential of ξ and S n (T, φ)
div.formula: = n-1 k=0 T k * φ is a Birkhoff sum. Note that 1 n 2 (ω + nξ + n(n-1) 2 π * η B (T, [ξ])) converges to 1 2 π * η B (T, [ξ]) in C 0 loc (X \ X 0
div.p: ). Under our assumption for contradiction, we have
div.formula: (5.5) 1 n 2 dd c (nφ -S n (T, φ)) → n→∞ 0 in C 0 loc (X \ X 0 ).
div.p: It suffices to show that Equation (5.5) is impossible. We interpret φ, the preferred potential of ξ = T * ω -ω, as follows: Since π is an elliptic fibration, each smooth fiber Proof. We consider a semi-stable reduction for the family π : (X, X 0 ) → (D, {0}) given by the following commutative diagram:
div.formula: Y X × D D t X D t D s µ p pr 1 pr 2 π f d
div.p: Here, f d (t) = t d = s is a base change of degree d, and µ is a resolution of singularities such that Y is a smooth complex surface. The singular fiber Y 0 = p -1 (X 0 ) is a reduced divisor with simple normal crossings. Let µ = pr 1 • µ.
Let m be the multiplicity of C in X 0 . Since µ * X 0 = dY 0 and Y 0 is reduced, the strict transform C of C appears in Y 0 with multiplicity 1. Thus, the pullback of C as a Cartier divisor is
div.formula: µ * C = d m C + k ν k E k
div.p: where E k are exceptional divisors. This induces a decomposition of the log-norm on Y :
div.formula: (A.1) log ∥ µ * σ∥ 2 µ * h = d m log σ C 2 h C + k ν k log ∥σ E k ∥ 2 h E k + u
div.p: where σ D denotes the canonical section of a component D ⊂ Y 0 , and u is a smooth function on Y . For t ̸ = 0, the map µ t : Y t ∼ -→ X s is an isomorphism. Pulling the integral back to the semi-stable model Y t , we obtain: Since Y 0 has strictly simple normal crossings, we apply Lemma A.2 to the first two terms of Equation (A.2), which yield:
div.formula: Yt log σ C 2 h C µ * α = C µ * α log |t| 2 + R 1 (t) = m C α log |t| 2 + R 1 (t) Yt log ∥σ E k ∥ 2 h E k µ * α = E k µ * α log |t| 2 + R 2,k (t), = µ * [E k ] α log |t| 2 + R 2,k (t) = R 2,k (t),
div.p: where R 1 (t), R 2,k (t) are Hölder continuous around 0.
Since u is smooth, by Barlet's result (Theorem 1.3), the third term (of Equation (A.2))
Yt u µ * α =: R 3 (t), is Hölder continuous around 0. Substituting the previous computations into Equation (A.2), we have extends Hölder continuously to t = 0.

Proof. Fix a finite open cover {U j } of Y 0 in Y by coordinate polydisks U j ∼ = D 2 such that on each U j , π is given either by t = z 1 (smooth points of Y 0 ) or t = z 1 z 2 (nodes of Y 0 ). Let {ρ j } be a smooth partition of unity subordinate to {U j }.

On each U j , we decompose the metric as log ∥σ D ∥ 2 h = log |f D | 2 +u j , where f D ∈ O(U j ) defines D ∩ U j and u j is a smooth function. By Barlet's theorem on fiber integral (Theorem 1.3), t → Yt∩U j ρ j u j ω extends Hölder continuously to t = 0. We analyze the singular integral I j (t) = Yt∩U j ρ j log |f D | 2 ω case by case:

Case 1: U j ∩ D = ∅. Here, f D is a non-vanishing holomorphic function on U j . Thus log |f D | 2 is smooth, and I j (t) is smooth around t = 0. In this case, I j (t) = D∩U j ρ j ω log |t| 2 + I j (t).

Case 2: U j intersects D outside the nodes. Here, π = z 1 and f D = z 1 . Thus I j (t) = log |t| 2 Yt∩U j ρ j ω. Since π on U j is a submersion, by Ehresmann's theorem and Taylor expansion, we have Yt∩U j ρ j ω = {z 1 =t}∩U j ρ j ω = {z 1 =0}∩U j ρ j ω + O(|t|) = Case 3: U j contains a node D ∩ D ′ . Here, the map is given by π = z 1 z 2 , with D defined by z 1 = 0 and D ′ defined by z 2 = 0. Thus f D = z 1 . The fiber Y t ∩ U j is the restricted annulus {(z 1 , z 2 ) ∈ U j | z 1 z 2 = t}.

Let η = ρ j ω. Since η is a smooth form with compact support in U j ∼ = D 2 , we can write it globally on the chart as η = i 2 2 k,l=1 η kl dz k ∧ dz l . We want to evaluate the asymptotic behavior of
div.formula: I j (t) = Yt∩U j log |z 1 | 2 η
div.p: We partition the domain of integration on the annulus Y t ∩ U j into two regions: V 1 (t) = {|t| 1/2 ≤ |z 1 | < 1} and V 2 (t) = {|t| 1/2 < |z 2 | < 1}.
On V 1 (t), we use z 1 as the coordinate. Substituting z 2 = t/z 1 and dz 2 = -(t/z 2 1 )dz 1 , the restriction of η is:
where the smooth coefficients η kl are evaluated at (z 1 , t/z 1 ). By the smoothness of η, we can Taylor expand the leading coefficient as η 11 (z 1 , t/z 1 ) = η 11 (z 1 , 0) + O(|t| / |z 1 |).

Integrating the cross-term and higher-order errors against log |z 1 | 2 over V 1 (t) yields bounded remainders. For example, passing to polar coordinates r = |z 1 |: On V 2 (t), we switch to the coordinate z 2 . Here, the log-norm splits exactly as log
Similarly, the restriction of η in the z 2 coordinate is:
div.formula: η| Yt = η 22 -
div.p: η 12 t z 2 2 -η 21 t z 2 2 + η 11 |t| 2 |z 2 | 4 i 2 dz 2 ∧ dz 2 with coefficients evaluated at (t/z 2 , z 2 ). The term involving -log |z 2 | 2 behaves exactly like the V 1 (t) integral, yielding a constant relative to t minus a remainder: -V 2 (t) log |z 2 | 2 η| Yt = -D∩U j log |z 2 | 2 η| D constant +O(|t| log 2 |t|) For the log |t| 2 term, we must integrate the volume-like form η| Yt over V 2 (t). Applying the exact same Taylor expansion and error bounds, the dominant term is η 22 (0, z 2 ). We have: V 2 (t) η| Yt = |t| 1/2 ≤|z 2 |<1 η 22 (0, z 2 ) i 2 dz 2 ∧ dz 2 + 1 |t| 1/2 O |t| r 2 r dr The error integral evaluates to O(|t| |log |t||). The difference between the main integral and the full integral over D ∩ U j is the missing disk {|z 2 | < |t| 1/2 }, which adds a simple O(|t|) error. Thus: V 2 (t) η| Yt = D∩U j η| D + O(|t| log |t|) Multiplying this volume result by log |t| 2 gives D∩U j ρ j ω log |t| 2 + O(|t| log 2 |t|). Combining both regions for the entire chart U j , we have I j (t) = D∩U j ρ j ω log |t| 2 + R j (t), where R j (t) = constant+O(|t| log 2 |t|) is Hölder continuous for any exponent 0 < α < 1 around t = 0. □

Therefore, Xs log ∥σ∥ 2 h α = C α log |s| 2 + R(s), and R(s) is Hölder continuous. We conclude that: Xs log ∥σ∥ 2 h α -C α log |s| 2 extends Hölder continuously through the origin. □ Lemma A.2. Let π : Y → D be a proper holomorphic map from a smooth complex surface to the unit disk. Assume the singular fiber Y 0 = π -1 (0) is a reduced divisor with strictly simple normal crossings. Let D be an irreducible component of Y 0 , σ D be its canonical section, and h be a smooth Hermitian metric on O(D). div.head: JUNYU CAO
div.p: X s is isomorphic to a torus C/Λ s , and T acts on X s by translations. Let ω flat,s be the normalized flat metric on X s . There exists ρ s ∈ C ∞ (X s ) such that ω flat,s -ω s = dd c ρ s .
Applying T * to this equation, we obtain ω flat,s -T * ω s = dd c T * ρ s , since ω flat,s is invariant under translation T . Then we have

Thus, the preferred potential φ of ξ is equal to T * ρ s -ρ s up to a constant on X s . By Ehresmann's theorem and solving equations on the region where the fibration is locally trivial, there exists ρ ∈ C ∞ (X \ X 0 ) such that ρ | Xs = ρ s on the smooth fibers
Substituting φ by T * ρ -ρ + c(s) in Equation (5.5), we have 1
By smoothness of ρ on X \ X 0 , the above convergence is equivalent to
We will show that Equation (5.6) is impossible.

On a small open set U ⊂ B • not containing any singular points, we can trivialize the fibration π as U × C/Λ → U . Moreover, we can lift the U × C/Λ to its universal covering U × C, and the fiber-preserving automorphism T can also be lifted, yielding the following commutative diagram:

We can then express the action of T on the covering space U s × C z as T (s, z) = (s, z + T (s)), where T : U s → C is a holomorphic function. Following the argument in [Can01, Proposition 2.2] (see also [Fil22, Proposition 6.2.7], [CD23a, Lemma 3.7]), the function T is non-constant.
Lifting the convergence in Equation (5.6) to the covering space, we find it is equivalent to
for all small open neighborhoods of points in B • and its corresponding covering, where ρ is the lift of ρ to the covering.

Thus, ω flat,s = ω s for s ∈ S • .
To get a contradiction, we compute the Gauss curvature of ω flat,s and ω on smooth fibers X s , and study them when X s is approaching a singular fiber.

By definition, the Gauss curvature K ω flat,s ≡ 0. On the other hand, by [DY25, Lemma 10.1], the minimum of K ωs goes to -∞ as s → 0. This leads to a contradiction, concluding the proof that the convergence is not in C 0 loc (X \ X 0 ). □ Appendix A. Asymptotics of a fiber integral

We prove a lemma on the asymptotics of the fiber integral of the log-norm of the canonical section of a divisor. This result also appears in other works (e.g., [Yos06, Section 4]); however, we provide a proof here in our setting for convenience and completeness.

Lemma A.1. Let π : X → D be a degeneration of algebraic curves. Assume X is smooth and we allow a general singular fiber X 0 here. Let C be one of the irreducible components of X 0 and L = O(C) the corresponding line bundle with Hermitian metric h.
Let σ be the canonical section of L which vanishes exactly on C. Then, for any smooth (1, 1)-form α on X, there exists a constant c ∈ Q >0 such that
